They are full-on compilations that have a clear beginning, middle and end to them, which I think hits harder than just a single clip,” Tess notes.

Sally Darr appreciated that the clips helped Kayda realize Alannah, who was removed from the villa after a video resurfaced of her using a racial slur while singing a song, did not participate in the Casa Amor recoupling.

“While I think Zach still would have chosen Kayda even if Alannah had been there for the recoupling, it’s strange that he never told her,” Sally Darr explains. “I suspected Zach hadn’t mentioned it, so I’m glad she finally knows now. Zach not telling Kayda was silly and has only made things worse.”
Read on to find out what Tess and Sally Darr thought about the islanders’ reaction to Part 1 of “Movie Night” and who they think is at risk of being dumped next.
Best Moment

Tess: “The best moment is watching Aniya and Melanie be there for each other, whether that’s like tonight in Movie Night or a couple episodes ago after the Casa recoupling. I’ve really enjoyed seeing their friendship develop and grow as the season’s gone on. Tonight when Melanie said she was shaking during Movie Night, and she wanted to hold Aniya’s hand, and she was there for her giving her the pep talk, I actually teared up a little bit. I thought it was so sweet, and it really goes to show that at the end of the day it kind of is ‘Friendship Island.’”

Sally Darr: “The best moment was Sincere’s Movie Night compilation. KC’s compilation was satisfying, too, but his Casa Amor behavior happened much more recently. Sincere lying to Melanie and Sol happened weeks ago, so revisiting it felt necessary.”
Biggest Surprise

Tess: “On a small scale, the biggest surprise was the editors leaving in two clips, following each other, of Sincere saying ‘beloved’ to both Melanie and Sol. I thought that was really funny. But I’m also surprised with the way that they went about structuring Movie Night. Normally, there will be little titles for the clips they’re about to play. There are no titles this year, it’s just doors. And it’s not just clips that they’re watching. They are full-on compilations that have a clear beginning, middle and end to them, which I think hits harder than just a single clip. And we’re not even halfway through. There is still a cliffhanger and more scenes to come, which I cannot wait to see.”

Sally Darr: The biggest surprise was Kayda finding out that Alannah left in the middle of Casa Amor. While I think Zach still would have chosen Kayda even if Alannah had been there for the recoupling, it’s strange that he never told her. I suspected Zach hadn’t mentioned it, so I’m glad she finally knows now. Zach not telling Kayda was silly and has only made things worse. It’s very short-sighted of him, since Kayda was always going to find out once she left the villa anyway.”
Best Couple

Tess: “The best couple for me is still Bryce and Trinity. They are so easy to root for. I loved seeing their date this week. Finally, we got a date! There have been no dates this season, except for the very first bombshell date. They’re already boyfriend and girlfriend, they skipped over the classic ‘Love Island’ step of closing off before getting to the boyfriend and girlfriend, which I think is really fun. But I do think that Carl and Aniya could catch up to them if they continue on this path with their connection, because it seems very sweet and genuine so far, and something that we would all want to root for, too.”
Sally Darr: “The best couple is Trinity and Bryce. They are going to win this season.”
Biggest Winner

Tess: “The biggest winner has to be Aniya. She has finally seen through all of the nonsense with KC and is able to start something fresh with Carl, who I think is a much better fit for her. It was really hard to watch her feel all that visceral sadness and emotion after the recoupling after Casa. So, I’m glad that she’s past that and able to continue on this new path with someone else.”

Sally Darr: “The biggest winner is Melanie. Even though she’s upset after watching Sincere’s compilation, she now knows she isn’t crazy. I hope she finally sees that Sincere doesn’t respect her, and ends things for good. She’s better off alone.”
At Risk of Being Dumped

Tess: “At risk of being dumped, I think, is most likely Corbin and Parmida, because I’m just not seeing a lot of people really root for them online. I think people don’t see Corbin as a positive influence in the villa, and Parmida, I don’t think is really resonating with viewers either. But I could also see KC and Titi getting the axe soon as well, because no one really wants to root for KC after everything that went down with Aniya. So, I guess one of those couples, or the individuals in those couples, might be going next.”

Sally Darr: “Jen is at risk of being dumped. She’s coupled up with Gal, but they’re no longer getting to know each other. Meanwhile, Gal is talking to Amora, which leaves Jen as the only single person in the villa right now.”

title: In ‘Elle,’ We Meet the Woman Who Made Elle Woods: Her Mother
summary: June Diane Raphael speaks about creating Eva, the mother of Elle Woods in the prequel series Elle, out July 1.
story: How did Elle Woods become Elle Woods? A new Amazon Prime prequel series explores the childhood that shaped the “Legally Blonde” character, and the mother that made her.

In “Legally Blonde,” the 2001 movie that started a phenomenon, Elle’s mom is just a tiny role. June Diane Raphael imagines Elle’s mom, named Eva, as a put-together, practical woman focused on the social ladder.

“It’s limited real estate, so in a way, for an actor, it’s so great because you can kind of create and build the character yourself,” June Diane Raphael said of the original character in an interview with TODAY
Elle, played by Lexi Minetree, gets more than just blond hair from her mom, but also her confidence and savviness.

Prior to landing “Elle,” Minetree’s biggest credit was a guest appearance on the Season 26 premiere of “Law & Order: Special Victims Unit.” She won over Reese Witherspoon, who originated the role and who produced the prequel through her company Hello Sunshine, with an audition tape.
With the exception of “Gilmore Girls,” Raphael tells TODAY.com most pop culture depictions of mother-daughter relationships don’t resonate with her.
“I think that so many representations of mothers and daughters in film and TV are a little odd,” Raphael says. “They’re filled with competition or weirdness.”
As Raphael explains, “Elle” turns that pattern on its head.
“I love playing this relationship that’s complicated and has its flaws, and sometimes they do the wrong thing, but it’s so fueled by love and admiration.”

Raphael has been vocal about what motherhood has meant to her, from the loss of her own mother when she was just 27 to raising her own two children, whom she shares with fellow comedian Paul Scheer. She tells TODAY.com what she hopes other mothers will take away from this expansion of a 90s film staple.

“My character rides so hard for her daughter,” Raphael says. “It’s really beautiful to see, and a lot of moms are going to resonate with that fact that we do anything, and sometimes that’s where we go wrong in a way, right?”

As a parent, Raphael thinks parents learn from kids if “you are awake from the experience.” Part of Season 1 sees Eva learning from her daughter and setting her up for Season 2, which she says allows Eva to explore who she is independent of her motherhood.

“So many women of that generation kind of looked up at a certain point, where the kids are getting older and maybe are a little bit more independent, and then they realize they’ve spent their whole life pouring everything into family and their husbands and kids, and then, what’s the next chapter for them?” Raphael says. “She realized a lot has been on hold, and so we get to explore what starts in the next chapter.”

title: Blake Lively Seeks $8 Million in Legal Fees in Justin Baldoni Dispute
summary: Blake Lively is seeking more than $8 million in attorneys’ fees and litigation costs from Justin Baldoni, his production company Wayfarer Studios, and the other parties she defeated in their now-dismissed $400 million defamation lawsuit.
story: Blake Lively is seeking more than $8 million in attorneys’ fees and litigation costs from Justin Baldoni, his production company Wayfarer Studios, and the other parties she defeated in their now-dismissed $400 million defamation lawsuit.

Lively accused Baldoni of sexual harassment during the production of their film“It Ends With Us,” which Baldoni directed and co-starred in. She also alleged that Wayfarer Studios retaliated against her after she complained about allegations of misconduct on the set of the film. Baldoni vehemently denied Lively’s claims.

The star first public with her allegations in December 2024, roughly four months after their film premiered in theaters. She first lodged a complaint with the California Civil Rights Department, then filed a federal lawsuit.

In a newly filed motion on Tuesday, Lively’s attorneys argue that Baldoni’s lawsuit was brought to punish Lively for reporting alleged sexual harassment and retaliation to California’s Civil Rights Department, rather than to pursue legitimate legal claims.

Her attorneys contend the defendants used the litigation to portray her as dishonest, intimidate potential witnesses, fuel a public relations campaign against her, and drive up the cost of her legal defense.

U.S. District Judge Lewis J. Liman tossed out most of Lively’s claims against Baldoni earlier this year, including harassment, defamation and conspiracy. The two parties reached a settlement deal in May.

Tuesday’s filing points to the court’s dismissal of the complaint, its finding that several claims were legally frivolous, and its ruling that Lively’s statements about the alleged misconduct were protected under California law.

Liman had dismissed Baldoni’s defamation and extortion lawsuit against Lively last year, but said she was allowed to move forward with a request for financial damages under California Civil Code Section 47.1, a law that protects people who report allegations of sexual misconduct from retaliatory defamation claims.

Lively’s legal team said the law requires reimbursement for the defense of the litigation because the legal work on the various claims was intertwined. They argue she is entitled to recover approximately $7,495,526.87 million in attorneys’ fees and another $539,514.01 in litigation expenses.

“Thanks to this landmark decision, those considering using a lawsuit as a weapon of intimidation have been put on notice that there are consequences for doing so,” Michael Gottlieb and Esra Hudson, Lively’s attorneys, said in a statement to NBC News. “The value of this ruling is in the precedent it creates, the accountability it imposes, and the protection it provides to those who may one day find themselves facing similar retaliation for speaking the truth.”

In the filing, the attorneys also defend the billing rates charged by her legal teams at Willkie Farr & Gallagher and Manatt, saying they were consistent with those typically charged in high-stakes, complex litigation.
The Wayfarer parties are expected to respond by July 13th.
Representatives for Baldoni did not immediately respond to request for comment on Tuesday.
This article first appeared on NBCNews.com.

title: What Kathie Lee Gifford Had to Say About Her Dating Life In New Interview
summary: For TODAY co-host Kathie Lee Gifford opened up in a new interview about romance and why she is enjoying single life in her 70s.
story: Kathie Lee Gifford is single and enjoying her life.

The longtime talk show personality and former TODAY co-host opened up in the cover story for the July 13 issue of People about how she views dating at 72 years old and the possibility of ever getting married again.

“I am not dating anybody right now, and I don’t want to date anybody,” she said. “I’m not looking, and I don’t think I’ll ever get married again. No, I don’t. I think I had an opportunity for that to maybe have happened, and it didn’t work out, so you move on. You stay friends if you can, and we have.”

Gifford said on TODAY in 2021 that she was in “a very special relationship” with a man whose name she did not divulge. However, Gifford shared with People in 2024 that she was no longer dating her mystery man.
She was previously married to Frank Gifford, a legendary New York Giants player and sports broadcaster, for 29 years before his death at 84 in 2015.
In her latest interview with People, also available on the People app, she did not completely shut the door on romance if the opportunity comes along.

“You know what it is? I leave room for the miraculous in my life. If God wants to do something miraculous to my life like give me another great love,” she said. “Frank will always be the love of my life. If you’re looking, then you’re looking for the love of the rest of your life.

“I need to find joy in the moment I’m in right now because none of us know when is our last breath,” she continued. “Instead of planning, ‘One day I’m going to take that vacation, one day this, my bucket list,’ that kind of thing, find joy right now in what you have already.”
The four-time Emmy winner and grandmother of five is more focused on what’s ahead.

“Because if you concentrate on everything you’ve lost and you live in the past — people who live in the past are doomed to die there,” she said. “And I don’t want to live in the past. I have been blessed for so many years, but I have great blessings today too, now that I’m back physically.”
Gifford shared that she had been in immense physical pain ahead of a hip replacement in 2024 and then pain in her other hip after she fell on Jan. 2 on the street outside her home.
She said she also broke her left arm while rolling over in bed in the middle of the night and underwent cataract surgery because her depth perception had been greatly affected.
“When I look back on all these years in this industry, I go, ‘I can’t believe I didn’t fall apart years ago,’” she said. “Your body starts to fall apart. And I never abused it, I just used it.”
She said she often stayed at home because she “didn’t want to go out like that” while experiencing chronic physical pain.
“I want to be Kathie Lee, the person they expect,” she said. “I don’t want to disappoint people.”
Gifford added that her vision is now 20/20 after cataract surgery, and she can be much more active with her grandchildren after stem cell treatments in her hip and arm.
“Now I’m in the season of the life where I don’t want to repeat myself,” she said. “I want to be remembered as somebody where if they only met me one time, she made me laugh, (and) she was kind.”

title: Read Paul Simon’s Letter for Clive Davis’ Funeral, Summing up 60 Years of Friendship
summary: A letter written by Paul Simon was read at the funeral for his close friend, music executive Clive Davis.
story: While Paul Simon wasn’t able to attend friend Clive Davis’ funeral, he made sure to honor the late music executive with a letter read aloud at the service.
Scores of stars turned out June 29 for the funeral of the legendary music executive, who died June 22 at the age of 94.

Kenny G performed a sax solo to kick off the event, held in New York City, while Dionne Warwick, Barry Manilow, Bruce Springsteen, Alicia Keys, Ja Rule and Stevie Wonder were also on hand, according to the Associated Press. Other luminaries, including Hoda Kotb, Adrien Brody, Nancy Pelosi and Gayle King, were also in attendance.

Davis, who won four Grammy Awards in his distinguished career, is known for launching and fostering the careers of some of the artists who attended his funeral as well as Whitney Houston, Aretha Franklin, Neil Diamond and more. He founded Arista Records and served as president of Columbia Records.

Simon, who was one of Davis’ longtime friends, wrote a letter that was read by Davis’ son Doug at the service, chronicling the relationship between the two musical heavyweights, both professionally and personally. Simon is currently on his “A Quiet Celebration Tour.”

“Beautiful moments happen when someone passes, if you can see them. One of those was reconnecting with that neighbor, Paul Simon,” Doug Davis said at the funeral, adding that his childhood best friend was Simon’s son Harper.

“I hadn’t spoken to Paul in 40 years. He and I have been able to speak every day this past week to reminisce and for me to receive some needed paternal advice and love,” Doug Davis added. “I finally had the chance to say something I’d wanted to say to him for 40 years. I told him when I was kid, he was just Harper’s dad. I had no idea the whole time he was Paul Simon.”
Read on for Simon’s full tribute to Clive Davis, provided to NBC News in full by the family.
Paul Simon’s Letter for Clive Davis’ Funeral

No one wants to find themselves speaking at a memorial for a dear friend. No one wants to piece together a chain of sentences that will inadequately express the shock of loss, the deep, sweet memories of times together, the love and admiration that was shared, and in my case, the closeness of our families.

When Doug called to tell me of his father’s passing, the name on my phone came up as Dougie Davis, Clive’s son. He then added that everyone in the world knows him now as Doug, but I would only think of him as Dougie, the kid who came to play with my son Harper in our apartment.

We were neighbors in the same building. His call made me think of the time Dougie and Harper were sitting at the kitchen table and l was mixing a chocolate milk for them. Harper accidentally tipped over Dougie’s glass. I said to him as l was cleaning up the mess, “You know, Harper, even if it’s an accident it’s still polite to tell your friend that you’re sorry.”
Harper said to Dougie, “Well I said, ‘Whoops-a-daisy.’”
I don’t think I ever told Clive that anecdote. So many stories, so many memories.

Clive and I were friends for nearly 60 years. Two New Yorkers from the boroughs who shared the kinship of a similar background. A lifelong friendship; always staying in touch and occasionally sharing a dinner or a summer afternoon.

With his loss, I now wish that we could have spent more time together. More time, more time. Comfortable, in each other’s company, conversation that was always interesting, ranging from music, to family, to politics and back to music.

Clive was a third of a trinity of record business deities: Mo Ostin, Ahmet Ertegun and Clive Davis. They shaped their labels around the artists. Leaders who changed the record industry. Men more interested in the music than popularity.

For a Harvard educated lawyer he had an uncanny grasp of the pulse of popular music. I remember the great anticipation that Art Garfunkel and I felt as we waited for Clive to hear “Bridge Over Troubled Water” for the first time. He sat immersed in the music and then turned to us and said, “That may be the most remarkable record I think I ever heard.” Or maybe I’m misremembering and he said, “What else you got?”
But I think he liked it.

A few weeks ago, my office got a message that Clive wanted to buy six seats to my upcoming concert at Forest Hills tennis stadium. The seats had to be dead center in the 10th row. When I play that night I’ll be singing for everyone, but in my heart I’ll be dedicating the night to the man in the empty seat, dead center in the 10th row.

title: ‘The Ring’ and ‘Lilo & Stitch’ Actor Daveigh Chase’s Cause of Death Revealed as AIDS
summary: The cause of death for Daveigh Chase, the actor known for her role in “The Ring” and voicing Lilo in the animated film “Lilo & Stitch,” has been revealed.
story: Daveigh Chase, the actor known for her role in “The Ring” and voicing Lilo in the animated film “Lilo & Stitch,” died from AIDS, according to the medical examiner report.

Chase, whose given name was Daveigh Schwallier, died in a Los Angeles hospital on June 16 at the age of 35. Her father, John Schwallier, confirmed to NBC News that she was homeless and living near the Los Angeles General Medical Center, where she died.
While The New York Times reported that Chase was living with her boyfriend, Schwallier said in a text message to NBC News that it was unclear what the man’s relationship was to his daughter.
“I don’t know how much of a boyfriend he was to her,” he said in a text message. “I seen a video of her in September. I don’t know why he wasn’t getting her medical help then.”
Daveigh Chase’s Cause of Death
Chase’s cause of death is listed as acquired immunodeficiency syndrome (AIDS), according to the Los Angeles County Medical Examiner’s report obtained by NBC News.
“Chronic polysubstance use” is also listed as a contributing factor. The report did not include what substances may have led to Chase’s death.
The manner is listed as natural. The case remains open.
Who is Daveigh Chase?
Chase was a child actor, whose first commercial was for Campbell’s Soup at age 7, according to IMDB.
Her breakout role was voicing Lilo in the animated film “Lilo & Stitch,” as well as the “Lilo & Stitch: The Series, “Stitch: The Movie” and various video games and spinoffs.

She was also known for being the child ghost Samira in “The Ring,” Samantha Darko in “Donnie Darko,” Rhonda Volmer in “Big Love” and had roles in “Mercy,” “CSI,” “Touched By An Angel” and “Family Law,” among others.
Her final acting credits are listed as 2016’s “American Romance” and “Jack Goes Home.”
What Is Acquired Immunodeficiency Syndrome (AIDS)?
Acquired Immunodeficiency Syndrome (AIDS) is a disease of the immune system caused by HIV, according to Clinicalinfo.

HIV (human immunodeficiency virus) is a virus that attacks cells that help fight infections, making the body vulnerable to life-threatening infections and cancers. If left untreated, it leads to AIDS.
Without medication, the lifespan of someone living with AIDS is about 3 years, HIV.gov reports. The time may be shorter depending on health factors.
What Is Chronic Polysubstance Use?
Chronic polysubstance use is when more two or more drugs are taken together, either intentionally or unintentionally, according to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC).

Intentional polysubstance happens when a person takes a drug to increase or decrease the effects of a different drug or wants to experience the effects of the combination, the CDC reports. When it happens unintentionally, the person consuming the drug does not know it has been mixed or cut with other substances.

title: Lexi Minetree, the World’s New Elle Woods, Loves Video Games and the Color Green
summary: Lexi Minetree talks becoming Elle Woods for new Prime Video show “Elle” and how she’s different from the iconic character.
story: When the camera cuts, the interview isn’t over. With Cut for Time, TODAY.com asks the burning questions our anchors couldn’t get to on-air.

For those who grew up in the ’90s, there’s a throwback series coming to Prime Video in all its pink glory. “Elle,” a new prequel series to “Legally Blonde,” also has evergreen lessons to offer younger generations — and not just how to use a landline.
Lexi Minetree, 25, takes the helm in the show, portraying Elle Woods years before her famous pivot to practicing law.

With a self-proclaimed adrenaline buzz from her first-ever morning show appearance, Minetree walks into TODAY.com’s Orange Room with an infectious smile and sports a hot pink blazer suit set. As we sit down for more questions after her on-air chat with Savannah Guthrie and Carson Daly, she compliments my own pink jacket, with a fluffy pen from the June 20 “Elle World” New York City pop-up event clipped to the collar.
She tells me she approached her role with one primary question: How did the Elle Woods of “Legally Blonde” first get her confidence? She says “Elle,” out July 1 on Prime Video, answers it.
This interview has been lightly edited for clarity.

I feel like with the prequel, you really get to explore how does one get to not only learn who they are, but how to gain the confidence to be different, and to own what makes you you. I think, especially nowadays, bringing that sort of empowerment to a new generation of women, as well as the people who grew up loving “Legally Blonde,” I think that’s really special. Now you get eight more hours of this amazing character.

She says at least once a week people come up to her and go, “I became a lawyer because of this role,” or, “This character changed my life, or empowered me, or made me embrace who I was,” and that means something when a character is that special to someone. I hold that it is important to take care of things like that, and I think she really wanted to make sure that we captured her spirit. I’m really lucky because she really trusted me with this character.

It’s so funny that you say that, because there’s this website that has factoids on people, and my friend said to me, because there’s  trivia on it, the facts about me are that she’s a big fan of SpongeBob. I feel like there‘s a big culture of nonchalant-ness now, where people are afraid to make bold choices or lean into their interests in fear of being judged. I feel like getting to play Elle, I got to lean into all those things that in high school maybe I would have tried to hide a bit, and be someone who just embraces who they are and leans into the cringiness.

I feel like most people go method for crazy roles, but I feel like this character is one of the best characters to go method for, leaning into her character traits, which is being hardworking and believing in yourself ... I also want this character so ingrained in me that when I get to set, I can be free to make my own choices, because the script is so fun and the other actors I work with are so talented, that we throw stuff at each other all the time. There’s so much improvisation that happens on set, it kind of makes me sad that viewers only get to see one take of everything, because we do some crazy stuff, that I’m like, “I know this will never make it into the show,” but we sometimes just do it to make each other laugh. But more than that, too, there is some really heartfelt moments every episode, and I feel like that is what is so special about the show.

Oh, I really like that question. Thank you for asking. I actually feel like I’m a very different person than this character. I myself am not nearly as poised and well-dressed as I am today. This is thanks to my stylist.

I think when you play any character, you want to bring some of yourself to it. So, I feel like I have also been able to infuse myself. I think I’m a pretty goofy person. I try to not take myself so seriously, but I also feel like I’ve learned a lot from her. She’s so confident, and I think, especially as a young woman, sometimes you have self-doubt. Sometimes, you’re like, “Can I do this? Am I good enough?” And so I honestly, I’m thinking I’m trying to become more like her as a human being.
And before this, I’d never done comedy before, I did only drama work, so this was such a wonderful challenge for me.
OK, research!

Yeah, I did “Law and Order SVU,” which felt like the christening of becoming a New Yorker, and my character’s arc at the end, she became a lawyer. This is before the open call came out, so lots of weird kismet things. Like, “Universe, what are you up to?”

title: The Best Books to Read This Summer, According to Isaac Fitzgerald and Yasmine Cheyenne
summary: Here are six beach reads to read in 2026 according to Isaac Fitzgerald and Yasmine Cheyenne.
story: Is it summer, or is it summer reading season? Both answers are correct. If you’re not sure where to start on the books front, Isaac Fitzgerald and Yasmine Cheyenne stopped by TODAY on June 29 to share a few of the books they’re loving this season.

Yasmine Cheyenne, who is also a wellness coach and a meditation expert, is the author of “The Comeback Era,” provided a few books that will transport you to a less stressful place (hopefully). One is the latest offering from a Read With Jenna author Jessica George.
"The Comeback Era" by Yasmine Cheyenne
Welcome to your comeback era.
Isaac Fitzgerald, meanwhile, is the author of memoirs and most recently the travelogue “American Rambler: Walking the Trail of Johnny Appleseed.”
"American Rambler: Walking the Trail of Johnny Appleseed" by Isaac Fitzgerald
A heartwarming novel that tells Fitzgerald's story about following the steps of Johnny Appleseed.
Below, check out their picks.
Isaac’s Picks
"Phoebe Berman's Gonna Lose It" by Brooke Averick
A funny, warm story about a hopeless romantic determined to lose her virginity before her 30th birthday.

What Isaac said: “Phoebe Berman’s Gonna Lose It’ is oh so warm and deeply charming — a novel about romance, friendship, anxiety, and the strange little deadlines we invent for becoming ourselves. In this case, well, losing one’s virginity before turning 30. Laugh-out-loud funny while still being tender and big-hearted, here is a love story in the truest sense, about learning to want honestly, messily, and entirely on your own terms.”
"Main Characters: A Novel" by Bobby Palmer
A love story of a pair whose paths unexpectedly cross throughout two decades, told from outside perspectives.

What Isaac said: “Main Characters’ is a brilliant book — a captivating love story told from the margins by the people who perhaps understand us before we understand ourselves. The book follows Clara and Seb across two decades as their lives brush up against one another’s, but is told from the perspective of everyone around them. A clever concept that totally works, here is a great summer read for anyone who has ever believed in the life-changing power of truly being seen.”
"This Vast Enterprise: A New History of Lewis & Clark" by Craig Fehrman
After years of research, this is an entertaining history of US history's two famous explorers.

What Isaac said: “An incredible feat, Craig Fehrman’s ‘This Vast Enterprise’ is a wildly entertaining history of Lewis and Clark — the product of more than half a decade of rigorous and immensely impressive research — that never loses sight of the adventure at its center. Fehrman brings this legendary expedition thrillingly to life by turning our attention beyond Lewis and Clark themselves, revealing the oft-overlooked people and perspectives that made the journey possible. Expansive, compelling, and alive on every page, this is a perfect summer read for America’s 250th anniversary.”
Yasmine’s Picks
"The Art of Loving You" by Natasha Bishop
The sequel to Bishop's "Only for the Week" of an intimate, spicy romance set during a bucket list road trip.

What Yasmine said: “Impossible to put down, this romance winds you through love, loss and the joy thats possible on the other side of change. If you love a plot that moves you as much as it inspires you, this is your pick!’
"Love by the Book: A Novel" by Jessica George
An honest story about the complications of adult friendships and new connections.

What Yasmine said: “A page-turner that puts words to what so many of us quietly feel about adult friendships — the growing pains, the unexpected shifts, and the beauty of who finds you in the in-between.”
"Waiting on a Friend: A Novel" by Natalie Adler
Natalie Adler's debut novel.

What Yasmine said: “A debut that somehow makes you feel more alive the deeper it pulls you into loss — an absolute love letter to a moment in history that still echoes today, with all the heart and humor to prove it.”

title: Former ‘Love Island USA’ Star Alannah Keyser Draws Backlash For Apology Video After Dismissal From Show
summary: “Love Island USA” fans criticize former contestant Alannah Keyser’s newest apology for her previous use of a racial slur.
story: “Love Island USA” fans don’t seem to be buying former contestant Alannah Keyser’s newest apology for her previous use of a racial slur, which ultimately led to her dismissal from the show.

In a TikTok video on June 27, Keyser addressed a resurfaced clip, in which she was seen using a racial slur while singing the lyrics to the 2019 Roddy Rich song “The Box.” Keyser told viewers that the video was “from six years ago, and that word is just not in my vocabulary anymore.”
“I’m sorry to whoever saw that video and has been offended by it,” Keyser said in the TikTok video. “That was never my intention.”
Keyser, a 21-year-old film student from Miami, also said that she doesn’t support “racism or discrimination of any kind, and I never have.”
“This has definitely been a learning lesson for me, and it sucks that I didn’t get a chance to really show my personality and who I am,” she said.
Peacock did not have further comment on the issue.
The video has received thousands of comments from viewers, many of whom said they found her apology insufficient.
“Genuinely the worst apology I’ve ever seen,” said one TikTok user, who received more than 12,000 likes for the comment.

Keyser, who joined the show as a Casa Amor bombshell during the June 21 episode of the steamy series, stopped appearing on the program after the June 25 episode, Peacock confirmed to TODAY.com. The new video is her first that addresses the controversy. Several fans criticized her TikTok apology, saying that the racial slur was never acceptable to use, and that she should have known better six years ago.
“To be saying that in 2020 is actually even more wild,” said another TikTok user.

Others took issue with the way in which she appeared to avoid kissing cast members KC Chandler and Sincere Rhea, who are both Black, during the show’s heart rate challenge. Keyser did not address her behavior in the challenge during the apology video, but wrote in the caption that her “chats/kisses with the other boys were unfortunately not aired.”
“this falls completely flat when we literally saw her discriminate against the black men on the cast. we have eyes girl,” one X user said.
Another X user wrote that “It’s not just the fact you said the word, it’s also how you visibly treated the black male contestants differently.”

Keyser is the latest in a string of “Love Island USA” cast members who were removed from the show after major controversies. Vasana Montgomery, for example, was featured in an introduction video for Season 8, only to be dismissed from the cast the next day after clips of her using a racial slur resurfaced. And in Season 7, cast members Yulissa Escobar and Cierra Ortega were both separately removed after their previous use of racial slurs came to light.

title: Josh Gad Honors ‘Comedy Idol’ Mel Brooks’ 100th Birthday With Emotional Instagram Post
summary: Josh Gad shared an Instagram post featuring his “comedy Idol” Mel Brooks for the legend’s 100th birthday on Sunday
story: Mel Brooks is entering his centennial year with some kind words of admiration from a fellow comedian.

Actor and singer Josh Gad shared some sweet photos on social media with Brooks in honor of the comedy legend’s big 100th birthday on June 28. Gad, who co-wrote the upcoming sequel to Brooks’ iconic 1987 “Star Wars” parody film “Spaceballs,” had some lovely things to say about the centenarian.

“For the past five years, I have had the greatest gift in my life… collaborating with and working alongside my comedy idol,” Gad wrote on Instagram of Brooks, who is also reprising his role as the beloved character Yogurt in the “Spaceballs” sequel. “Today we celebrate 100 years of his life and legacy. He has made us laugh and hurt on the big screen, the small screen, the stage and even on our record players.”
Gad added: “There is nothing he hasn’t won, nothing he hasn’t achieved, nothing he hasn’t proven and yet at 100, he continues to surprise and thrill us.”

The series of 20 photos posted to Gad’s social media features snapshots from his time working with Brooks on the “Spaceballs” sequel. In nearly every shot, Brooks, known for his joyful disposition, is grinning from ear-to-ear.

Citing a number of Brooks’ ongoing projects including the West End production of the legend’s iconic musical ‘The Producers’ and the upcoming television adaptation of 1974 hit “Young Frankenstein,” Gad wrote that the ‘best is still yet to come’ for the 100-year-old.
“I love you Mel. I cannot wait for the world to see this next chapter,” Gad wrote. “Thank you for your trust and your partnership.”
Brooks, who was born in 1926 in Brooklyn, is critically acclaimed for his work at the helm of a number of boundary-pushing films. He previously credited his longevity to his sense of humor.
“It’s an amazing sound, people laughing at something I created,” Brooks told People in January. “Making comedy is a great job. It keeps you sane and happy. It gives you a reason to be alive.”

title: ‘Pineapple Street’ Author Jenny Jackson Shares the Opening Lines of Her Next Book — and the Real Incident that Inspired Them
summary: Jenny Jackson shares the real life incident behind the opening line of her second book “The Shampoo Effect.”
story: It is a truth universally acknowledged that a good story starts with a good first line. In TODAY’s Cold Open, authors tease their buzzy upcoming release by revealing and explaining its first line, paragraph or page.
“Pineapple Street” author Jenny Jackson always knew how she was going to start her second book, “The Shampoo Effect.”
“That was always going to be the opening line,” she tells TODAY.com.
The book’s first words were borrowed directly from an incident in her sister-in-law’s life that she can’t stop laughing about.

In the first scene, Caroline Lash, a New Yorker who takes a writing residency in Massachusetts, steps on a doughnut and gets some help with clean-up from a stranger, whom she later learns is named Van. It’s an instant meet-cute.

It could have been a Boston Cream, puffy white, the inside sweet vanilla pudding, but instead it was a jelly donut, glazed with sugar, filled with the sort of fluorescent red goo that could only be created in a food science lab. Caroline didn't even realize she'd stepped on the thing.
She says something similar happened to her sister-in-law: “She is the nicest human in the world, and the stuff that happens to her, you wouldn’t believe.”
Once, she was running late to a job interview in Boston and “stomped” on a jelly doughnut, per Jackson’s retelling.
“She goes to the lobby of the job interview and realizes there is red jelly all over her pants,” she says.
She had to make a quick outfit change using a pashmina she happened to have in her bag.
“She takes off her pants, shoves them in a bag, wraps a pashmina around her waist like a skirt, and does the whole job interview wearing like a hippie pashmina skirt,” Jackson says through laughter.

“Every time I think about it, I get the giggles. I have been unable to stop laughing about this for so long. So when I thought about Boston jelly doughnuts, it had to be the opening scene for the book,” she continues.
Jackson says her sister-in-law doesn’t know about the scene yet. “I’m so excited to give it to her. She’s going to freak out.”

It was the rest of the book that was a challenge. “The Shampoo Effect,” chosen as Read With Jenna’s July 2026 pick, is a departure from the rarefied Brooklyn world that her bestselling debut “Pineapple Street” was set.

Jackson, who is also the editorial director of fiction at Knopf, says she “couldn’t get comfortable” with the idea that people associated her with the characters from “Pineapple Street,” which features an affluent Brooklyn family.
“I’m not a one percenter, and I didn’t grow up in New York. Part of writing ‘The Shampoo Effect’ for me was subconsciously a corrective to write about my hometown and where I’m from,” she says.
She wanted to write a book for people who already liked “Pineapple Street” and people who didn’t, which she calls a “terrible way” to go into a novel.
The book is set in a similar coastal Massachusetts town to where she grew up, Ipswich, and follows a childhood friend group now in their 30s.
She calls the book “a story of love and marriage, and that moment in your early 30s where you have your real coming of age.”
To create the friend group, she “borrowed bits and pieces from a zillion sort of characters” that she grew up with — beyond just her sister-in-law.
For example, she recalls a childhood friend whose father was a beer distributor and lived in a fancy house — which makes it into the book through the character of Bailey.
Her husband is the inspiration for Van. “He read the book, and he was lying in bed. He was like, ‘Really, honey?’ I’m like, ‘Sorry,’” she says.
The heart of the book is inspired by the “fascinating thing that happens” when your old friends start to take different life paths.

“One person settles down first, one person leans in professionally first, one person has a baby first. It’s easy to that as a judgment on yourself and your own decisions,” she says. “You innately want your friends to be on your same page. It keeps you in conversation with each other, and also sort of cosigns your life choices.”

title: New Details Emerge About Prince Harry and Meghan Markle’s UK Visit in July
summary: Prince Harry and Meghan Markle will be traveling to the United Kingdom in July. Their children will also join them.
story: Prince Harry and Meghan Markle will soon travel to the United Kingdom for a special trip that will include appearances at multiple public events.

The couple, who stepped away from their duties as senior members of the British royal family in 2021, will fly across the pond next month with 7-year-old Prince Archie and 5-year-old Princess Lilibet, a source confirmed to NBC News.

Prince Harry and Markle, officially the Duchess of Sussex, have been raising their children in Montecito, California, for the past few years. The family will return to his homeland and from July 7 to July 11 will celebrate one year until the Invictus Games are held in Birmingham, England. British media first reported news of the U.K. visit.

In addition to promoting the July 2027 games, which are a multi-day sporting competition, Prince Harry is also set to make appearances at charity events for WellChild and Scotty’s Little Soldiers, the source tells NBC News. Prince Archie and Princess Lilibet will not be in attendance at any of the public events and the couple does not plan to have media interviews.

Buckingham Palace did not confirm to NBC News if Prince Harry and Markle will reunite with King Charles III during their stay. But, the source told NBC News that the Sussexes were offered housing at a royal residence for the duration of their visit.
The family will stay for some of their time in the U.K. in a royal residence, the source confirmed to NBC News.

As for the family’s security protection while abroad, a government spokesperson told NBC News, “It is our long-standing policy not to provide detailed information on those arrangements, as doing so could compromise their integrity and affect individuals’ security.”

Last year, Prince Harry reunited with his father for the first time in 19 months when he flew home in September 2025. The father and son spent time at the king’s residence, Clarence House, in London and had a private tea, Buckingham Palace officials confirmed at the time.

Prince Harry was in London to reconnect with some charities and organizations he partners with. The solo visit came after he shared in a May 2025 interview with the BBC that he wanted to mend the fractured relationship with his family.

“I would love reconciliation with my family. There’s no point in continuing to fight anymore. Life is precious. I don’t know how much longer my father has. It would be nice to reconcile,” he said at the time.

Prince Harry was interviewed after he lost an appeal to get his British security detail restored. He said the decision was a “surprise” and he was “devastated,” noting that he needed a higher level of security to bring his wife and children to the U.K.
“I can’t see a world in which I would be bringing my wife and children back to the U.K. at this point. And the things that they’re going to miss are, well, everything,” he shared.

He continued, “I love my country, I always have done, despite what some people in that country have done ... and I think that it’s really quite sad that I won’t be able to show my children my homeland.”
Markle showed a glimpse into their life as a family of four in the states recently when she posted a rare photo on Instagram of her husband and children to celebrate Father’s Day.
Below the snap, which showed Prince Archie and Princess Lilibet embracing their father, she wrote, “They’re so lucky to have you. We all are.”

title: USWNT Star Jaedyn Shaw Shares Her World Cup Memories and Career Moment She’d Like to Relive
summary: Jaedyn Shaw shares her World Cup memories, as well as what she’s most looking forward to as the tournament is underway and more.
story: Jaedyn Shaw remembers being a kid, not knowing if her dreams of being a soccer star would pan out, “but having the belief” that it could happen.
The 21-year-old United States Women’s National Team (USWNT) star tells TODAY.com that when training as a young girl, she understood the sacrifices it took “to achieve my dream.”

At 17, Shaw signed with the National Women’s Soccer League, kick-starting her professional career. Now, with a FIFA U-20 Women’s World Cup and an Olympic gold medal under her belt, she’s looking forward to watching the United States Men’s National Team score big wins during the 2026 FIFA World Cup, where she’ll be a spectator of the international tournament for the first time.

“To see a World Cup on our home soil is something that’s also really special. It’s so cool to see all of the players that we’ve seen growing up on TV, being in the most random cities in the country,” the Adidas athlete says.
Below, in her own words, Shaw shares her World Cup memories, as well as what she’s most looking forward to with the tournament underway.
I’m going to have to go with the 2015 Women’s World Cup. Carli Lloyd getting a hat trick from half field. That’s probably my biggest World Cup memory.

I was with the U.S. National Team, playing in my home city, Frisco, in the FC Dallas Stadium, where I grew up going to games and training. I play for FC Gotham right now. We ended up going down a goal and we ended up coming back, and I scored the winning goal in my home city, home state, basically in front of all of my friends and family that came. That moment is probably a moment that I’ll remember forever.

It’s going be really exciting to watch the U.S. Men’s National Team play. It’ll be really cool to see how they come out mentally. I think they have the ability to go very far in this tournament, and of course I want them to do well. Also, seeing (Lionel) Messi playing in Alabama, they (were) staying in Kansas City in the hotels that we stay in as well. It’s really crazy to see that they’re training on the same pitches, playing the same stadiums that some of us will play on, or have played in. It’s really cool to have the opportunity to watch.

title: ‘Love Island USA’s’ Sol Dean Reveals the Couple She’s ‘Glad to Be Wrong’ About
summary: “Love Island USA” Season 8 bombshell Sol Mýa Dean’s time in Fiji was short but tumultuous — entering the villa on Day 7 and being dumped on Day 15.
story: “Love Island USA” Season 8 bombshell Sol Mýa Dean’s time in Fiji was short but tumultuous — entering the villa on Day 7 and being dumped on Day 15.
The Southern California-native quickly formed a connection with Sincere Rhea upon entering the villa and coupled up with him on Day 10, per America’s vote.
Their connection was cut short just four episodes later, when Sincere chose to rekindle his relationship with Melanie Moreno during the “You’ve Got Mail” challenge.

The shake-up left Sol coupled up with fellow bombshell Caleb McDaniel by default, but the pair only spent one day together before being voted among the least popular couples. That night, Sol was selected by a group of male islanders to be sent home alongside Gabriel Vasconcelos, who entered the villa as a bombshell on Day 2.
Upon hearing the news of the vote, Sol told the islanders that no one seemed open to getting to know her when she arrived, and she thanked the cast for putting her out of her “misery.”
Rather than shedding tears on her way out the door, she paused a final time to tell the islanders to make sure future newcomers don’t feel “excluded” like she did in the villa.
Below, Sol shares more about what really went down — from the emotional pie challenge to Sincere’s speech at her dumping — and which islander she thinks should win it all.
This interview has been lightly edited for clarity.

I think it was a lot of stuff. I don’t know, it was just rough going in there, it was a lot. There was a lot that was just happening, and all I’m gonna say is that it was more than two. But it was just emotional, like hearing them pick me for answers that they didn’t really get to know me for. I feel like that was my big thing. I just came in, they knew my name is Sol, and I’m a bombshell, and nothing else, so I just kind of felt a little dissed where I’m like, where are these warranted when no one’s gotten to know me?

Maybe applying more pressure with the guys. But I also want things to feel mutual, and if they’re happy and content where they’re at, then I’m not going to overly want to pursue something. So it was hard, kind of finding the silver lining of what I should do and what I shouldn’t do, but I felt like (with) the experience I did the best with the cards I was dealt.
I don’t know if he wanted to get back for me humbling him, but I was just like, OK, this is like the finale cherry on the top of you being able to just kind of dump me.

I don’t know, I didn’t feel like the situation was fair, honestly, and him saying that I wasn’t in a progressive couple when he knows that I obviously put most of my time and energy into getting to know him, because what he was telling me versus what he was saying to other people was different. But me, I’m someone that likes to take things at face value, and I mean, I won’t really have doubt until there’s a reason, so I mean, I was feeling like a bunch of different ways.
At least, I don’t know, someone else could have said it.

I just feel like I bit my tongue a lot, honestly, to keep things cordial and to have peace for living with these people 24/7. It’s also being filmed, so you’re kind of trying to navigate things in the best way, and I don’t really know if they knew like the effect that they were doing to me in the villa. And I feel like that’s just more so something I want to touch on when they do come out, and how they made that not a wonderful experience for me … There’s no shade. I hope a lot of them are still in their couples, but do I want to explain how they were making me feel while my time in the villa was very short and present there? I do want to kind of address that with them. So, that’s mainly my focus for that.

The hate on Trinity is a super misconception. On “Aftersun,” I feel like a lot of people kind of misconstrued my words, but in the beginning I said I wanted to get to know Trinity more because she was hilarious. She’s funny as hell. I felt like after watching some of the episodes, too, it just kind of made me validate the feelings I was feeling in the villa about what was being said and why there was just like, random tension. But again, I feel like the misconception would be Trinity and what I said on “Aftersun.”

I only said what I said on “Aftersun” because Bryce did pull Kayda multiple times and get rejected. It’s just kind of like, in my mind, if I’m keeping it plain simple at face value of what I’m seeing, if you were happy with your couple, you wouldn’t want to keep exploring, so that was just kind of more my premise on that. I didn’t say I think that she’s prettier than her. I could have obviously delivered it better, and I feel like that’s something I need to work on ... There’s no hate on Trinity. I think she’s amazing, and honestly, out of everyone, I think she should win this, so I’ll stand on that.

I haven’t really watched Casa Amor, I only watched like the first episode, but like, the little clips I have been seeing, I just saw both of them kind of like crying, I believe for each other, and I’m glad I’m wrong.

When you’re in the villa, like, a day feels like a month, and I already know that they had a connection brewing, and I wasn’t saying that they didn’t have a connection brewing, it was more so, like, Bryce just kept pulling Kayda and got rejected, so it was like, I didn’t say that they didn’t have a connection. But I’m honestly glad, I’m glad to even see this pan out. I think it’s great. I’m glad to be wrong on that, I do feel like they have a connection.

I feel like it’ll make the girls just more open, if anything, and I feel like that was maybe the point. The girls are super locked in and just super forgiving with their guys, but if you got double the men, and you just saw how your current person was moving, like it’s, “Go do your big one, girl,” you know. Go find someone else in Casa and just give other people a fair shot. So I hope that they’re doing that, honestly.

I kind of knew the boys were gonna go crazy in Casa, just since they’re not being watched. So, I don’t know if I could boil it down to just one person. I couldn’t really answer that, but I feel like all of them kind of just felt like the chains were being broken loose. It was like a brand new show happening again, having a bunch of people get introduced, you get to know everyone without, like, a current partner watching, or like reprimanding you for it. I don’t know, I feel like they all were kind of, like, really excited from what I saw.

Watching it from a viewer’s perspective is interesting. It’s a little cringe, but it’s also interesting because I’m like, completely myself all the time, and I feel like, again, you guys get one hour out of 24. Watching it back is kind of just like, there’s a lot of things that just were happening that I wasn’t aware of, so maybe asking like questions more, I feel like, again, I take a lot of things at face value, so maybe kind of trying to get the scoop, and what’s going on. Why is blank upset, or whatever it is. It’s interesting watching yourself back.

title: Melanie Martinez Reveals Name of Next Album
summary: Melanie Martinez has revealed exclusively on TODAY the name of her upcoming album will be “Elysium.”
story: Melanie Martinez has already revealed the name of her next album, just three months after the release of “Hades.”
The former “Voice” contestant dropped the news exclusively on TODAY June 26, saying her 2026 album, “Hades” is Part 1 of a two-album effort.
“There’s another album that I haven’t said the name yet, but maybe I’ll say it now. Why not?” the “Dollhouse” singer said as the crowd on the plaza went wild.
“The second album is called ‘Elysium.’ That’s all I’ll say, but it’s a utopian reference, so it’s the other part. It’s the counterpart to it,” she added, as the crowd continued howling.
While she didn’t reveal too much information about the album itself, she did talk about how “Hades,” which topped the Billboard rock and alternative albums chart in April, came together.

“With ‘Hades,’ it was just kind of reflecting on the current times, and the world feels very dystopian, so I wanted to make a dystopian record,” she said, noting she was inspired by the book “Parable of the Sower” by Octavia E. Butler.
“I thought it was time to make an album about all of the topics that I felt were really kind of heavy on my heart and I’m just really happy that resonate with it,” she said.
Martinez has quite the loyal fanbase, which has grown since she appeared on “The Voice” in 2012. It’s a rise that has surprised her.
“I’m always in awe, and unbelievably grateful,” she said.

“And I just feel lucky that I get to do what I love and that I get to put on shows and create safe spaces for people all over the world to come together who are like-minded and creative and passionate,” she added. “And it’s inspiring and I just want to do it for the rest of my life, so I’m just really grateful.”

title: ‘Love Island USA’ Removes Another Contestant, Alannah Keyser, For Using Racial Slur
summary: “Love Island USA” contestant Alannah Keyser will no longer appear on the program after a video of her using a racial slur surfaced.
story: “Love Island USA” contestant Alannah Keyser, who joined the show on the June 21 episode of the hit Peacock series, will no longer appear on the program following the June 25 episode, the streaming service has confirmed to TODAY.com.
Her removal comes after a video resurfaced in which she used a racial slur while singing the lyrics to the 2019 Roddy Rich song “The Box.”
Keyser, 21, hails from Miami and had served as one of the Casa Armor bombshells. She appeared to have made a connection with Zach Georgiou.

“Love Island USA” has removed other cast members before. Vasana Montgomery was among the islanders who appeared in an introduction video for Season 8 on May 28. Clips of her using a racial slur surfaced shortly after that and she was dismissed from the cast the next day. She did not appear on the June 2 premiere.

A pair of Season 7 islanders were also removed from the villa in 2025. Yulissa Escobar appeared in one episode before viewers were informed that she was no longer on the show after clips of her using racial slurs on a podcast came to light.

“In those clips, I used a word I never should’ve used, a racial slur,” she later wrote, in part, on Instagram. “I used it ignorantly, not fully understanding the weight, history, or pain behind it. I wasn’t trying to be offensive or harmful, but I recognize now that intention doesn’t excuse impact. And the impact of that word is real. It’s tied to generations of trauma, and it is not mine to use.”
Cierra Ortega, who was also on Season 7, left the show toward the end of that season after videos of her using racial slurs surfaced.
She later apologized in a TikTok video.

“I completely agree with the network’s decision to remove me from the villa. I think that this is something that deserved punishment, and the punishment is absolutely been received, for sure,” she said.
Escobar and Ortega departed the show under similar circumstances, which Escobar noted.
“I’m not justifying what I did, I know what I did and what I said was wrong, and I know what Cierra said was wrong and I know it hurt communities,” she said about Ortega in a TikTok video.

title: U.S. and Iran set for new talks, Trump says, after Hormuz clashes threatened peace deal
summary: Trump said the two sides would meet Tuesday in Doha, Qatar. Hours earlier a senior Iranian official denied such negotiations were set to take place.
story: JERUSALEM — The United States and Iran will hold new talks Tuesday in Qatar, President Donald Trump said Monday, after the two sides appeared to step back from a wave of attacks that threatened to derail peace efforts.
Limited time: Save 25% on NBC News subscription
Get exclusive reporting, live Q&As and ad-free reading.
“IRAN HAS REQUESTED A MEETING. IT WILL TAKE PLACE TOMORROW IN DOHA!” Trump said on Truth Social.

White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt later said special envoy Steve Witkoff and Trump’s son-in-law Jared Kushner would be flying to Doha for “high-level meetings” held alongside “technical talks.”
There was no immediate reaction from Tehran. Hours earlier, a senior Iranian official denied any technical discussions were scheduled to take place.
Technical teams working on the implementation of the initial agreement between the two sides are scheduled to meet in Doha in the coming days, a source with knowledge of the talks told NBC News.
Communications channels created to de-escalate any incidents are in place and technical talks are set to continue, the source added.
This follows a series of escalating reciprocal strikes over the weekend that threatened to unravel efforts to secure a permanent end to the war.

The clashes were sparked by efforts to reopen the Strait of Hormuz without Iranian oversight, which drew angry warnings and attacks from Tehran as it fights to maintain its grip on the crucial waterway.

Days of public dispute over the terms of the deal — both around the future of the key trade route and the unfreezing of billions in Iranian assets — escalated into new military attacks after Iran attacked ships transiting the strait.

Bahrain and Kuwait came under Iranian attacks early Sunday, hours after the U.S. military said it had hit multiple targets across Iran in response to “continued aggression” against commercial shipping.
Tehran threatened a “complete halt” to negotiations if Washington continued its attacks.
Trump warned Saturday on Truth Social that “there may come a point when we are no longer able to be reasonable, and will be forced to militarily complete the job that we very successfully started.”
The clash centers on control over the Strait of Hormuz, which Iran says it retains under the Memorandum of Understanding signed with the U.S.
The establishment of a new United Nations-backed route near Oman for inbound and outbound traffic led to an uptick in ships making it through the strait last week.

title: Sen. Roger Marshall says U.S. strikes on Iran are a ‘mop-up operation’
summary: The Kansas senator’s comments came hours after the U.S. launched fresh strikes and Tehran targeted sites in Bahrain and Kuwait.
story: Sen. Roger Marshall, R-Kan., said Sunday that the war between the U.S. and Iran has moved into a stage he called “a mop-up operation” hours after the U.S. launched fresh strikes on Iran.
Limited time: Save 25% on NBC News subscription
Get exclusive reporting, live Q&As and ad-free reading.

“The major war is over, and think of this as almost just a mop-up operation,” Marshall said on NBC News’ “Meet the Press.” “We have to press them if they strike us. We have to strike them back by 10.”

The two nations signed a memorandum of understanding last week paving a path to end the conflict. But Saturday, U.S. Central Command said the U.S. launched retaliatory strikes on Iran for the second time in two days. The first time came after Iran struck a ship moving through the Strait of Hormuz. The second time came after the Defense Department said Iran struck “targets linked to the American aggressor forces” in the region.

Marshall also pointed to a framework agreement for peace between Israel and Lebanon that was brokered last week by Secretary of State Marco Rubio as evidence that the U.S. was moving toward reaching its goals, which Marshall characterized as “no nukes for Iran, no forever wars,” adding, “Bring the cost of gas and grocery down.”
He added: “Let’s root for the Trump team for once, rather than try to tear them down. So I think we’re doing what we need to do, making great progress here and again working toward those goals.”

Pressed about whether the war is actually over, Marshall said: “I’m asking America to hang in there. This is a, this is a détente. This is a ceasefire, and yeah, they broke the ceasefire. We have to answer that, as well.”

Marshall also dismissed concerns that President Donald Trump was backing down from his promises to make life more affordable for Americans by halting the signing of a major bipartisan housing affordability bill.

Trump committed not to sign that bill, which was passed by the House and the Senate, until Congress passes the SAVE America Act, legislation that would overhaul elections by requiring voters to present their IDs at the polls and handing state voter rolls to the federal government to check for noncitizens.

“Housing is part of the puzzle, but this is classic Donald Trump negotiation style,” Marshall said. “He’s going to grab every leverage point he can, and this is one to get the bigger priority to cross the finish line, and that’s election integrity.”
“We can have both,” he added. “I understand the cost of living is the defining issue right now, but also ... everyone back home is really concerned about election integrity.”

title: Iran strikes Kuwait and Bahrain as escalating attacks threaten to unravel peace efforts
summary: President Donald Trump has warned the U.S. could be forced to return to war as a series of escalating reciprocal strikes with Iran continued into Sunday.
story: President Donald Trump has warned that the United States could be forced to return to war as a series of escalating reciprocal strikes with Iran continued into Sunday, threatening to unravel peace efforts between the two countries.
Limited time: Save 25% on NBC News subscription
Get exclusive reporting, live Q&As and ad-free reading.

Bahrain and Kuwait faced fresh Iranian attacks early Sunday, hours after U.S. Central Command said it had hit multiple targets across Iran in response to “continued aggression” against commercial shipping in the Strait of Hormuz.

“United States aircraft just struck Iranian missile and drone storage locations, and coastal radar sites, for violating the Cease Fire Agreement, AGAIN!” President Donald Trump said Saturday on Truth Social.

“It is very possible that they will never learn! There may come a point when we are no longer able to be reasonable, and will be forced to militarily complete the job that we very successfully started. If that happens, the Islamic Republic of Iran will no longer exist!”
CENTCOM said in a statement Saturday that U.S. fighter jets conducted strikes on 10 Iranian military targets at multiple locations.
Iran was “given a chance to honor the ceasefire agreement but elected not to when its forces launched a one-way attack drone,” it said, referring to an attack on a Panama-flagged vessel Saturday.
Meanwhile, Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps said it had launched more missiles and drones at U.S. military infrastructure in Kuwait and Bahrain.
American bases “will experience hell in these coming days,” the IRGC navy command said, as reported by Iran’s semiofficial Fars news agency.
Bahrain, which hosts the U.S. Navy’s 5th Fleet, said Sunday that a residential building was damaged by the overnight attacks, with “no loss of life.”
The country’s Foreign Affairs Ministry expressed its “strongest condemnation” of Iranian attacks on the territory, asserting its right to defend itself.

“This dangerous escalation reveals that Tehran’s actions are not isolated incidents or random acts, but rather a deliberate and systematic pattern of repeated aggression against the Kingdom’s sovereignty and the security of its citizens and resident,” it said in a statement on X.
Kuwait’s army said Sunday that its air defenses were also countering Iranian missile and drone attacks. Two ballistic missiles were intercepted, it said, with no damage reported.

The series of reciprocal attacks began last week with a disagreement over shipping in the Strait of Hormuz. Tehran, which had committed to reopen the critical waterway, has insisted that vessels follow a specified route close to its coastline, but many vessels are choosing instead to transit through the waters of the United Arab Emirates and Oman.
Is the Strait of Hormuz open or closed?

After a series of Iranian threats to vessels that had used the alternative route, a Singapore-flagged ship was attacked Thursday, to which the U.S. reciprocated by hitting Iranian military targets. Those strikes triggered an Iranian response, continuing the cycle of attacks that has since escalated.

Revolutionary Guard spokesman Hossein Mohebi said Sunday that “every time the enemy violates the ceasefire, it will receive a harsher response than before,” according to Iran’s hard-line Student News Network.
The Revolutionary Guard also said it would deal with vessels it claims violated its approved route “stronger than before.”
Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi said later Sunday that the Strait of Hormuz will be managed under Iranian administration and “will return to pre-war capacity within 30 days.”
“No other country or entity has responsibility in this regard and any interference or attempt otherwise will complicate the situation,” he said during a trip to Baghdad, the capital of Iraq.

Vice President JD Vance, who had been tapped to play a lead role in ongoing U.S.-Iran negotiations, said Friday on X: “If they have disagreements about how the MOU is being applied, they can pick up the phone. But violence will be met with violence.”

The memorandum of understanding the U.S. and Iran signed less than two weeks ago stipulates that the Strait of Hormuz — a vital waterway through which 20% of the world’s oil passed before the war — will reopen. It said Iran would “make arrangements” to allow the safe passage of commercial vessels with no charge for 60 days.
But both sides have repeatedly accused the other of breaching the terms of the deal.
The memorandum of understanding is “vague and ambiguous,” said Fawaz Gerges, a professor of international relations at the London School of Economics.
The deal stipulates that Iran “must make arrangements and best efforts to allow vessels to sail in the Strait of Hormuz,” he said, but the U.S. “does not really accept Iran’s designated route.”

That illuminates “a great deal about the vast gulf that exists between the United States and Iran,” he said. “If they cannot peacefully manage a 60-day ceasefire, how can they resolve the huge differences regarding Iran’s nuclear program and a lasting settlement?”
U.S. allies in the Persian Gulf, including Egypt, Saudi Arabia, Qatar and Jordan, condemned Iran’s latest attacks on Bahrain and Kuwait and called for restraint.
Qatar “emphasizes the necessity of sparing the region the consequences of these unjustified attacks, continuing on the path of dialogue and diplomacy,” its Foreign Affairs Ministry said Sunday on X.
“I think we don’t have a full ceasefire, a complete ceasefire. We have a lesser ceasefire,” Gerges said. “I wonder whether the new normal is a limbo state of no war and no peace.”

title: South Africa says over 900 arrested during nationwide anti-migrant protests
summary: Thousands have fled South Africa, fearing they will be attacked or killed, after an anti-immigrant movement set a “deadline” for them to leave the country.
story: JOHANNESBURG — South African police said more than 900 people were arrested on Tuesday, when ​there were nationwide anti-migrant protests that were mostly ​peaceful but occasionally descended into violence and looting.
Limited time: Save 25% on NBC News subscription
Get exclusive reporting, live Q&As and ad-free reading.

Tebello ⁠Mosikili, a deputy national police commissioner, told ​a press conference that of the 120 marches ​that took place on Tuesday, 108 were peaceful while 12 needed law enforcement to intervene due to unrest.
Some of those ​arrested were undocumented migrants detained for violating ​immigration rules, while others were arrested for public violence, harboring ‌illegal immigrants and robbery.
Mosikili said police reinforcements had been deployed to five of the country’s nine provinces overnight to respond to isolated incidents of looting and ​criminality.
South Africa braces for anti-immigrant protests
Soldiers were ​sent to ⁠Johannesburg’s Hillbrow neighborhood to support the police.
Tuesday’s marches were organized to mark ​a “deadline” an anti-immigrant movement had set for ​undocumented ⁠migrants to leave South Africa.
The protests came after months of unrest that have drawn international criticism as ⁠foreigners ​have been driven from their ​homes and seen their businesses and property vandalized.

The most prominent groups opposing illegal immigration include March and March, Operation Dudula and Progressive Forces. President Cyril Ramaphosa, who has challenged the protesters’ allegation that migrants are to blame for the country’s social and economic challenges, met Monday night with leaders of some of the groups and asked them to conduct peaceful demonstrations.

Ngizwe Mchunu, one of the protest leaders, told The Associated Press that he blamed illegal migration for a proliferation of illicit drugs in South Africa. He also complained about the high percentage of informal neighborhood shops run by immigrants from other African countries, saying they should all be owned by South Africans.

“It’s a very sad story that we have been telling our government since the dawn of democracy that illegal immigration here is out of hand,” Mchunu said. “It is time for our government to put South Africa first.”

Amnesty International South Africa said migrants, refugees and asylum seekers are being unfairly blamed for the country’s unemployment, inequality and struggling public services, arguing that those challenges stem from the legacy of apartheid, persistent inequality and failures in the asylum system.

title: Ocean surface temperatures hit record high, fanning scientists’ concerns of global crisis
summary: “The planet is warming because we’re emitting vast quantities of greenhouse gases, primarily from fossil fuel burning,” one expert said.
story: Temperatures on the ocean surface hit a record high in June, European scientists warned Wednesday, fueling fears of more dangerous heat waves this summer and fanning concerns over the escalating global climate crisis.
Limited time: Save 25% on NBC News subscription
Get exclusive reporting, live Q&As and ad-free reading.

Two separate services under the European Union’s Copernicus Earth observation program — the Copernicus Climate Change Service and the Copernicus Marine Service — announced they had both independently confirmed the record temperatures.
Carlo Buontempo, director of the Copernicus Climate Change Service, warned that the rising temperatures could mark the “beginning of a new phase.”

“With ocean temperatures at these levels and El Niño on the horizon, we are likely to see more temperature records fall in the coming months,” Buontempo said in a statement Wednesday. El Niño is a naturally occurring climate cycle that sees the central and eastern tropical Pacific Ocean become warmer than usual for months at a time.
The world could be heading into “uncharted territory,” Buontempo warned.

title: Video of a newborn and his mother rescued after days under rubble offers hope in Venezuela
summary: An initial estimate of 50,000 unaccounted for is “terrifyingly plausible,” according to the United Nations.
story: Rescuers crowd around a newborn wearing only a diaper and bundled in a striped blanket in a video that offers a glimmer of hope to families whose loved ones are still missing after Venezuela’s huge earthquakes last week.
Limited time: Save 25% on NBC News subscription
Get exclusive reporting, live Q&As and ad-free reading.

The footage published by The Associated Press showing 18-day-old Juan David and his mother, Dayana Patino, being saved from the rubble after their 32-hour ordeal in the northern coastal region of La Guaira was shared around the world this week.
“You are tough, Mami, you are tough,” a voice in Spanish tells a smiling Patino while she is still trapped under the rubble.

Speaking with British broadcaster BBC News from her hospital bed at a clinic in the Venezuelan capital, Caracas, on Sunday, Patino said she had been washing dishes in her eighth-floor apartment when the earthquakes hit.
“I don’t know how I didn’t let go of my baby because I was flying,” she said, adding: “I got crushed against furniture.”
She said she believed it was her determination to keep her baby safe that kept her alive.
“As long as he was alive, I was going to be alive,” she said.

The twin earthquakes, which struck last Wednesday, devastated parts of Venezuela, with at least 1,943 people killed and at least 10,571 injured as of Tuesday and thousands still missing. Hopes were fading that survivors might still be found.

The United Nations said Monday it had agreed with Venezuelan authorities to procure 10,000 body bags in anticipation of the death toll rising further. It said officials had yet to confirm a figure for the missing, but U.N. Emergency Relief Coordinator Tom Fletcher said an initial estimate of 50,000 unaccounted for was “terrifyingly plausible.”
According to the U.N., more than 5,000 people have also been injured and 12,000 displaced.
On Monday, interim President Delcy Rodríguez said authorities continued “efforts to rescue survivors, which fills us with emotion and hope for their families and for our people.”

International teams have continued to flood into the country in recent days to boost the search efforts, with the critical 72-hour window for rescuing people still trapped beneath the rubble having passed.

title: Russian satellite communications center hit, Ukraine says, as drones target Moscow
summary: The governor of the Moscow region said a 6-month-old died and three people were injured in the “large-scale attack.”
story: More than 60 drones targeted Moscow overnight, the city’s mayor said Tuesday, marking another major aerial attack on the Russian capital as Ukraine intensifies its long-range weapons campaign.
Limited time: Save 25% on NBC News subscription
Get exclusive reporting, live Q&As and ad-free reading.

A satellite communications center was hit, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy said on X, adding that it was used for reconnaissance and for ​coordinating the activities of Russia’s forces in Ukraine.
Describing it as a “large-scale attack,” Mayor Sergey Sobyanin said in a post on Telegram that air defenses had “destroyed 61 drones approaching Moscow,” without specifying where they had come from.
Emergency services were working at crash sites but did not report any casualties, he said.

The assault on Moscow was part of a broader overnight wave of drone attacks across Russia. The Russian Defense Ministry said air defenses shot down 419 Ukrainian drones over 18 regions, including the Russian-annexed Crimean Peninsula off southern Ukraine.

A 6-month-old baby died when a drone crashed into a private home in Yegoryevsk, a small town around 100 miles to the southeast of the Russian capital, Dmitry Vikulov, the head of the municipal district of the same name, said in a statement. He added that three adults and two children were injured.
Andrey Vorobyov, the governor of the Moscow region, said on his own Telegram channel that an administrative building in Dubna, about 69 miles north of Moscow, was damaged by falling drone debris.
The attacks also briefly disrupted operations at Moscow’s Domodedovo and Zhukovsky airports, according to Russia’s federal aviation agency, Rosaviatsiya.

Zelenskyy said on X that his military struck the Dubna satellite communications center ​in the Moscow region. He added that the facility, which is around 310 miles from the Ukrainian border, ​was used for reconnaissance and for ​coordinating the activities of Russia’s forces in Ukraine.
Additional strikes were to follow. “Relevant actions are also being prepared against other similar enemy facilities,” he said.

The latest Ukrainian barrage came less than two weeks after the country hit a major oil refinery in the Moscow region in what appeared to be its largest aerial assault on the Russian capital since Moscow’s full-scale invasion in 2022.
Kyiv described those strikes as retaliation for Russian attacks on a historic monastery complex in the Ukrainian capital this month.

Rodion Miroshnik, Russia’s envoy for what it calls the “crimes of the Kyiv regime,” said on Telegram Tuesday that the number of civilian casualties from Ukrainian attacks had exceeded 300 people in a single week for the first time this year. He appeared to be referring to a total number of killed and injured.
